title: Chapter 36: Scattered
content: The tunnel shook. Dust rained from the ceiling and Wren's hands hit the ground, palms scraping concrete, her glitch still rippling outward like a dying heartbeat. The camera at the junction fuzzed — static, snow, nothing — and then snapped back.
Too slow. She was getting too slow.

"Move," Ash said. His hand closed around her wrist and hauled her up. The tunnel stretched ahead, black and reeking of rust and standing water. Somewhere behind them, the Hounds' servos whined — that high, thin sound that burrowed into her molars.
She ran.

They had lost the others in the first collapse. Silas had been ahead — or behind — she couldn't remember now. The explosion had torn the Underlayer apart like wet paper, and Lira's voice still echoed in Wren's skull, that flat calm saying *I had no choice*, and then the Hounds pouring in through the gaps. Twelve, fifteen, twenty machines with sensor arrays rotating and tranquilizer chambers cycling and that terrible patient efficiency that never stopped.
They'd run. That was all they could do.

The abandoned subway line sloped downward. Water pooled ankle-deep, gray and chemical-bitter. Wren's boots splashed through it and the cold cut through the fabric, climbed her shins. She kept her glitch going on the next camera — a red-eyed thing mounted above a service ladder — and felt the pull behind her navel, that sucking drain like blood leaving her head.
The camera fuzzed. Held.
She let it go and stumbled. Ash caught her elbow.
"Easy."
"I'm fine."
He didn't argue. That was worse than if he had.

They moved through the dark. Ash's lighter flicked on — a small orange flame cupped in his palm, casting shadows that jumped and staggered across the curved walls. The tunnels here were pre-war, old city infrastructure that NeuroScale had never bothered to map. Good. Meant the Hounds wouldn't have it either.
But the Hounds didn't need maps. They had heat signatures. They had learning algorithms. They had patience.

Wren's glitch had bought them time. Every camera between the Underlayer and here — she'd hit them all. Scrambled feeds, overwritten data, noise where her heat print should have been. But the effort stacked inside her like stones on her chest. Her vision kept swimming at the edges. Her fingers tingled.
"How much further?" she asked.
"Depends on what's further."
"Helpful."
"I try."

The tunnel branched. Ash stopped, flame raised, reading the faded signage on the wall. SECTOR 7 — SIGNAL MAINTENANCE. An arrow pointed left, toward a door that had been welded shut decades ago and then pried open more recently — scratch marks around the frame, the hinges black with age.
Ash shouldered through first. Wren followed.

The signal room was small. Concrete walls, a rusted control desk, dead monitors stacked like headstones. The ceiling was low enough that Ash had to duck. But it was dry. The door had a lock — mechanical, not digital, which meant no cameras inside. No networked systems. A blind spot in the city's nervous system.
They were, for the first time in hours, unwatched.

Ash slid down the wall and sat hard. His breath came through his teeth in controlled pushes — in through the nose, out through the mouth. Training. Discipline. But Wren could see the cost on him now. The sweat on his jaw. The trembling in his hands.
And the wound.

She saw it properly for the first time in the dim light of his lighter. His left shoulder — four parallel gouges running from collarbone to bicep, deep enough that she could see the muscle moving underneath. Not bleeding anymore, but ragged. Raw. The sensor array he'd ripped off the Hound had ripped back.
"You're hurt," she said.
"I noticed."
"Let me see."

He didn't move. Just watched her with that steady unreadable expression she was learning to read anyway. The tension in his jaw meant he was in pain. The slight angle of his body meant he was protecting the left side.

She knelt beside him. Her hands were shaking but she made them steady — forced them steady — and unzipped the med kit from his pack. Gauze. Antiseptic wipes. No anesthetic. They'd used the last of it on Keel, two days ago. A lifetime ago.
"This is going to sting," she said.
"Already does."

She cleaned the wound. The antiseptic hissed against raw flesh and Ash hissed with it, a sharp intake through his teeth. His hand came up — not to stop her, but to grip the edge of the desk beside him, knuckles white. The tattoos on his forearm shifted with the tension, and in the faint orange light, they pulsed.
Not a trick of the flame. The lines — geometric, interlocking, code-like — seemed to brighten from within. A soft blue luminescence that flickered and faded.
Wren paused. "Your tattoos."
"I know."
"They're—"
"I know." His voice was flat. Not angry. Tired.

She wanted to ask. But there was a wall in his tone, and she was too exhausted to climb it. Instead she wrapped the gauze — tight, firm, layer over layer — and tied it off. Her fingers brushed his skin. Warm. Real. Alive.
He released the desk. His hand dropped.
"Thanks," he said.
"Don't mention it."
The silence that followed wasn't comfortable. But it wasn't hostile either. It was the silence of two people who had run out of running and were sitting in the aftermath, breathing.

Wren leaned against the wall beside him. The concrete was cold through her jacket. Her body ached — every joint swollen, every muscle fiber screaming. The glitch had drained something fundamental from her. Not just energy. Something deeper. Like she'd spent reserves she didn't know she had.
She thought about the others. Silas. Keel. Mira. The old man they called Docket who had taught her how to move through the Underlayer without sound. Scattered. Maybe captured. Maybe dead.
She thought about Lira. The calm voice. *I had no choice.*
There was always a choice.

"I can't keep this up," Wren said. The words came out before she could stop them. Not a confession. Just a fact. "The glitching. Every time I do it, it takes more out of me. And I have to do it more often because there are more cameras, more Hounds, more—"
"Then stop doing it alone."
She turned her head. Ash was looking at her — not at the wound he'd just let her dress, but at her face. His expression had shifted. Less guarded. The wall down, just a crack.
"I'm the only one who can do it," she said.
"No. You're the only one who does it because you think you have to carry everything." He shifted, winced. "You don't. I'm here. Slower, louder, more scarred — but I'm here."
The room was dark except for the dying lighter. Ash killed the flame and the black was absolute. Wren heard his breathing. Her own. The distant drip of water somewhere in the tunnels.
She thought about the Signal Key in her pack. The subject files on the drive. Twelve names. Twelve lives consumed by NeuroScale's machines. She was the thirteenth. Lucky number.
His tattoos pulsed again in the dark — a brief blue glow through the blackness, like a heartbeat made of light. In and out. In and out. Then gone.
Wren closed her own eyes and let the darkness take her.
They were alive. They were together.
It had to count for something.
